title: Sparse & Efficient: MoE + MLA
description: Advanced efficiency techniques for trillion-parameter models. Learn how Mixture of Experts routes computation sparsely, and how Multi-head Latent Attention compresses KV tensors. Deep dive into DeepSeek V3 and Mixtral architectures, plus the modern serving stack (vLLM, llama.cpp, MLX), speculative decoding, and evaluation harnesses.

sections.title: Mixture of Experts (MoE)
sections.content:
Transformers scale quadratically in attention compute, but the FFN dominates training FLOPs (typically 60–70% of the per-layer cost in a dense model). Naive scaling — make every FFN bigger — pushes you to 100B+ dense parameters and weeks of training on thousands of accelerators. Mixture of Experts (MoE) is the dominant alternative: keep a huge total parameter count for capacity, but only activate a small fraction per token. **The idea.** Replace the single dense FFN with `N` parallel "expert" FFNs and a tiny router. For each token, the router picks the top-`k` experts to run (typically `k=2` or `k=8`); the rest are skipped entirely. Only `k/N` of the FFN parameters are active per token, but the model can specialize: different experts learn different patterns (code, math, language, etc.) and the router learns which experts handle which tokens. **Routing mechanism.** For each token embedding `x`: 1. **Router logits.** A small linear layer produces logits over the `N` experts: `g = W_router · x` of shape `(N,)`. 2. **Top-k gating.** Take softmax, then keep only the top `k` entries (zeroing the rest) and renormalize. The gate values are the mixture weights. 3. **Dispatch and combine.** Send the token to its top-`k` experts, run their FFNs, and combine the outputs as `y = Σᵢ pᵢ · Eᵢ(x)` where `pᵢ` is the renormalized gate weight. ```mermaid flowchart LR T1["token x"] --> R["Router
(linear → softmax)"] R --> S["scores over N experts"] S --> TK["top-k selection (k=2)"] TK -- "p_a" --> EA["Expert A (FFN)"] TK -- "p_b" --> EB["Expert B (FFN)"] EA --> SUM(("Σ")) EB --> SUM SUM --> Y["y = p_a·E_A(x) + p_b·E_B(x)"] AUX["Aux load-balance loss
(across all tokens)"] -. regularizes .-> R ``` **Active vs total parameters.** This is the headline number for MoE. With `N=128` experts of 1B params and `k=2`, you get **128B total params but only ~2B active per token** — 1.6%. The model has the *knowledge capacity* of 128B but the *inference cost* of a 2B dense model, plus the router and a small overhead for dispatch. ```cudadiagram MoEParamBudget ``` **The load balancing problem.** Without intervention, the router collapses: it keeps picking a few favorite experts, and the rest ("dead experts") never receive gradients and never improve. The fix is an **auxiliary loss** that penalizes imbalance. The standard formulation (Switch Transformer, GShard) uses a soft, differentiable proxy: - `importance[e] = mean over tokens of router_probs[e]` — how much soft mass goes to expert `e`. - `load[e] = fraction of tokens whose top-k included expert e` — how often it actually fires. - `aux_loss = α · top_k · num_experts · (load · importance).mean()` When routing is uniform, `load = importance = 1/N` for every expert and the term equals `top_k · 1/N`. When the router collapses to one expert, `load·importance` for that expert is large and the loss spikes. The product form is differentiable through `importance` (the router probs), giving the router a usable gradient — counting `top_k_indices` directly is non-differentiable and only works as a metric. **Top-2 vs top-k tradeoffs.** Top-2 (Mixtral, GShard) keeps the dispatch volume modest — every token reaches exactly 2 experts — and is cheap to all-to-all across GPUs. Top-1 (Switch Transformer) is even cheaper but trains less stably; top-8 (DeepSeek-V3, with many fine-grained experts) gives more mixing but doubles communication. Practical rule: top-2 if you have 8–32 experts of moderate size, top-6 to top-8 if you have 64–256 fine-grained experts. **Expert capacity and token dropping.** In a real distributed MoE, each expert is pinned to a GPU ("expert parallelism"), and tokens are routed via an all-to-all collective. Each expert has a fixed **capacity** = `(tokens_per_batch / num_experts) · capacity_factor` (typically `1.0` to `1.25`). If routing sends too many tokens to one expert and exceeds capacity, the overflow is **dropped** (their FFN output is zero, the residual connection still passes through). Dropping is what makes the kernel a fixed-shape dense GEMM per expert; the alternative — variable shapes — is far slower. Capacity factor `1.0` drops aggressively; `2.0` wastes compute. Tuning this is one of the practitioner-y parts of training MoEs. **Real numbers (DeepSeek-V3, late 2024).** 671B total parameters, 37B active per token (5.5% activation), 256 routed experts + 1 always-on shared expert, top-8 routing per layer. Training cost: ~$5.6M on 2048 H800s (vs ~$70M+ for an equivalently capable dense model). It famously **drops the auxiliary loss** entirely and instead adds a learned per-expert bias to the router logits, nudged each step toward balancing — simpler and avoids the gradient interference of an aux term. Mixtral 8x7B (Jan 2024) sits at the simpler end: 8 experts, top-2, ~46.7B total / ~12.9B active. Both ship as open weights. **When to use MoE in practice.** Use it when (a) you have the compute to train it (MoE training is communication-heavy; needs fast interconnect like NVLink/InfiniBand), (b) you have enough VRAM to *serve* the full model (all expert weights must be in memory even though only 2 fire per token — this is the catch), and (c) your batch is large enough to keep experts busy (low batch sizes hit load-imbalance tail latencies hard). Don't use it for single-user low-batch chat inference on a single GPU — the active-params win is real but the memory tax is brutal. MoE shines for multi-tenant serving with heavy throughput.
sections.code:
import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import torch.nn.functional as F

class Expert(nn.Module):
    """Single expert: small FFN."""
    def __init__(self, d_model, d_ff):
        super().__init__()
        self.w1 = nn.Linear(d_model, d_ff)
        self.w2 = nn.Linear(d_ff, d_model)

    def forward(self, x):
        return self.w2(F.gelu(self.w1(x)))

class MoELayer(nn.Module):
    """Mixture of Experts with top-k routing and a *correct* differentiable
    load-balance auxiliary loss (Switch Transformer / GShard form).
    """
    def __init__(self, d_model, num_experts, expert_dim, top_k,
                 load_balance_loss_weight=0.01):
        super().__init__()
        self.num_experts = num_experts
        self.top_k = top_k
        self.load_balance_loss_weight = load_balance_loss_weight

        self.experts = nn.ModuleList([
            Expert(d_model, expert_dim) for _ in range(num_experts)
        ])
        self.router = nn.Linear(d_model, num_experts, bias=False)
        self.aux_loss = torch.tensor(0.0)

    def forward(self, x):
        """
        x: (batch, seq_len, d_model)
        returns: (batch, seq_len, d_model)
        """
        batch, seq_len, d_model = x.shape
        x_flat = x.view(-1, d_model)                       # (T, d)
        T = x_flat.size(0)

        # 1. Router logits and probabilities
        router_logits = self.router(x_flat)                # (T, N)
        router_probs = F.softmax(router_logits, dim=-1)    # (T, N)

        # 2. Top-k selection and renormalization
        top_k_probs, top_k_idx = torch.topk(router_probs, self.top_k, dim=-1)
        top_k_probs = top_k_probs / top_k_probs.sum(dim=-1, keepdim=True)

        # 3. Dispatch tokens to experts and combine outputs
        out = torch.zeros_like(x_flat)
        for k in range(self.top_k):
            for e in range(self.num_experts):
                mask = (top_k_idx[:, k] == e)
                if mask.any():
                    out[mask] += top_k_probs[mask, k:k+1] * self.experts[e](x_flat[mask])

        # 4. Differentiable load-balance auxiliary loss.
        #    importance[e] = mean router prob across tokens (soft, differentiable)
        #    load[e]       = fraction of tokens whose top-k included expert e
        #    aux           = top_k * N * sum_e (load[e] * importance[e])
        #
        #    At uniform routing this equals top_k * (1/N) and is minimized;
        #    at collapse it spikes. Gradients flow through `importance`.
        importance = router_probs.mean(dim=0)              # (N,)
        # one-hot of top-k indices, then mean over tokens
        topk_mask = F.one_hot(top_k_idx, num_classes=self.num_experts).sum(dim=1)  # (T, N)
        load = topk_mask.float().mean(dim=0)               # (N,)
        aux_loss = self.top_k * self.num_experts * (load * importance).sum()
        self.aux_loss = self.load_balance_loss_weight * aux_loss

        return out.view(batch, seq_len, d_model)


# ---- demo ----
torch.manual_seed(0)
d_model, num_experts, expert_dim, top_k = 256, 8, 512, 2
moe = MoELayer(d_model, num_experts, expert_dim, top_k)
x = torch.randn(4, 10, d_model)
y = moe(x)

print(f"output shape       : {y.shape}")
print(f"aux load-bal loss  : {moe.aux_loss.item():.6f}")

# Parameter accounting
expert_params = sum(p.numel() for p in moe.experts.parameters())
router_params = sum(p.numel() for p in moe.router.parameters())
active_per_token = top_k * sum(p.numel() for p in moe.experts[0].parameters()) + router_params
print(f"total expert params: {expert_params/1e6:.2f}M")
print(f"active per token   : {(top_k/num_experts):.1%} ({active_per_token/1e6:.2f}M)")

# Sanity: when routing collapses, aux_loss > top_k/N; when uniform, ≈ top_k/N.
# With 8 experts, top_k=2, the floor is 2/8 = 0.25 (before the weight scaling).

references.title: Outrageously Large Neural Networks: The Sparsely-Gated MoE (Shazeer et al., 2017)
Switch Transformer (Fedus et al., 2021) — top-1 routing + load-balance loss
GShard (Lepikhin et al., 2020) — capacity factor, top-2 routing
DeepSeek-V3 Technical Report — fine-grained experts, aux-loss-free balancing

sections.title: Multi-head Latent Attention (MLA)
sections.content:
The KV cache grows linearly with sequence length and quadratically with batch: shape `(B, T, n_h, d_k)` for K, same for V. At inference with long contexts and big batches, the KV cache — not the weights — dominates GPU memory. For a Llama-style model with `n_h=32, d_k=128, d_model=4096`, every cached token costs `2 · 32 · 128 · 2 bytes ≈ 16 KB` (fp16 K + V). At batch 32, sequence 8192, that's **~4 GB just for cache**, more than the weights of a 7B model. GQA helps (Module 8) but only by ~4–8×. MLA goes much further.

**Idea.** Don't cache full K and V. Instead, project the input down to a small **shared latent** `c_kv` (e.g., 512-dim) and cache only that. K and V are reconstructed from `c_kv` by up-projection only when attention needs them.

```cudadiagram
MLACompression
```

**The naive (training-time) view.**

1. Compute Q normally: `Q = x · W_q`, shape `(B, T, n_h·d_k)`.
2. Compress: `c_kv = x · W_dkv`, shape `(B, T, d_c)` with `d_c << n_h·d_k`. **Cache `c_kv`.**
3. Up-project at attention time: `K = c_kv · W_uk`, `V = c_kv · W_uv`.
4. Standard attention `softmax(QKᵀ/√d) · V`.

If `d_c = 512` and `n_h·d_k = 4096`, the per-token cache is `512` floats instead of `2 · 4096 = 8192` floats — **16× smaller** before counting GQA-equivalent factors. DeepSeek-V3 reports ~5–10× compression vs an already-tight MQA baseline.

**The catch the simplified version misses: at inference you don't actually want to up-project.** If you naively run the steps above during decoding, you reconstruct full K and V from `c_kv` every step — same FLOPs as standard attention, you just saved memory bandwidth on the KV reads. The whole win of MLA comes from a trick called **"matrix absorption"**.

**The absorb trick (the actual point of MLA).** Note that the score for query head `h` against key position `t` is:

```
score_{h,t} = q_h · k_h(t)
            = (x_q · W_q^(h)) · (c_kv(t) · W_uk^(h))ᵀ
            = x_q · (W_q^(h) · W_uk^(h)ᵀ) · c_kv(t)ᵀ
```

The two weight matrices `W_q^(h)` and `W_uk^(h)` can be **multiplied offline** into a single combined projection `W_qk^(h) = W_q^(h) · W_uk^(h)ᵀ`. Then at inference:

- `q_latent_h = x_q · W_qk^(h)` — projects the query directly into latent space.
- score against position `t` is just `q_latent_h · c_kv(t)`.

**No K is ever materialized.** The same trick absorbs `W_uv` into the output projection so V never materializes either. The latents `c_kv(t)` stay compressed all the way through attention; you only ever do `(d_c)`-sized dot products instead of `(n_h·d_k)`-sized ones. This is what makes MLA *both* memory-efficient and compute-efficient at decode time — and why our naive implementation below, which decompresses every step, captures the memory savings but not the compute savings.

**RoPE complication and DeepSeek's fix.** RoPE rotates K and Q by position-dependent matrices. If we absorb `W_uk` into Q's projection, that absorption is position-independent — RoPE would have to rotate the *latent* `c_kv`, which doesn't have the right structure for the rotation. DeepSeek's solution: split each head into two channels, a **non-RoPE** channel (most of `d_k`) that goes through the latent and the absorb trick, and a small **decoupled RoPE** channel (e.g., 64 dims) that bypasses compression — RoPE is applied to a separate, uncompressed K_rope cached alongside `c_kv`. The two contributions are summed in the attention score. This adds a small per-token overhead (`2 · 64` floats for K_rope) but lets the absorb trick work on the bulk of the head dim.

**Trade-off.** Compression introduces a low-rank bottleneck — there is information loss. DeepSeek-V2/V3 found the loss is negligible for generation quality and is more than compensated for by the larger batches and longer contexts the saved memory enables. The latent is learned end-to-end so the model can choose what to keep.

**Adoption.** DeepSeek-V2 (May 2024) introduced MLA; DeepSeek-V3 (Dec 2024) refined it. Combined with MoE, it's how DeepSeek fits a 671B-param model into reasonable inference budgets. Most other 2024–2025 architectures still use GQA (Llama 3, Qwen, Mistral) — MLA is more invasive to implement and requires the absorb trick to be a clear win, so it hasn't been universally adopted yet, but it's the most aggressive KV-cache compression technique currently in production.
sections.code:
import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import torch.nn.functional as F

class MultiHeadLatentAttention(nn.Module):
    """Simplified MLA: shared compressed K/V latent, up-projected at attention time.

    NOTE: this implementation decompresses every forward call, so it shows the
    *memory* win (cache only c_kv) but not the *compute* win. Production MLA uses
    matrix absorption: W_uk is folded into W_q so the latent is never expanded.
    See the prose for a full derivation.
    """
    def __init__(self, d_model, n_heads, d_latent=128, d_rope=32):
        super().__init__()
        assert d_model % n_heads == 0
        self.n_heads = n_heads
        self.head_dim = d_model // n_heads
        self.d_latent = d_latent
        self.d_rope = d_rope                        # decoupled-RoPE channel

        # Q projection: split into a non-RoPE bulk and a RoPE channel
        self.q_proj = nn.Linear(d_model, n_heads * self.head_dim)
        self.q_rope_proj = nn.Linear(d_model, n_heads * d_rope)

        # Shared K/V latent (one matrix, fed to two up-projections)
        self.kv_down = nn.Linear(d_model, d_latent, bias=False)
        self.k_up    = nn.Linear(d_latent, n_heads * self.head_dim, bias=False)
        self.v_up    = nn.Linear(d_latent, n_heads * self.head_dim, bias=False)
        # Decoupled RoPE K (uncompressed, small)
        self.k_rope_proj = nn.Linear(d_model, d_rope, bias=False)  # shared across heads

        self.out_proj = nn.Linear(n_heads * self.head_dim, d_model)

    def forward(self, x, mask=None, cache=None):
        """
        x: (B, T, d_model)
        cache: dict with 'c_kv' (B, T_past, d_latent) and 'k_rope' (B, T_past, d_rope)
        returns (output, new_cache)
        """
        B, T, _ = x.shape
        H, D = self.n_heads, self.head_dim

        # Q (non-RoPE bulk + small RoPE channel)
        q = self.q_proj(x).view(B, T, H, D)
        q_rope = self.q_rope_proj(x).view(B, T, H, self.d_rope)
        # (RoPE rotation on q_rope and k_rope omitted for brevity — see Module 8.)

        # KV latent (this is what we cache)
        c_kv = self.kv_down(x)                     # (B, T, d_latent)
        k_rope = self.k_rope_proj(x)               # (B, T, d_rope), shared across heads

        if cache is not None:
            c_kv   = torch.cat([cache['c_kv'],   c_kv],   dim=1)
            k_rope = torch.cat([cache['k_rope'], k_rope], dim=1)
        new_cache = {'c_kv': c_kv, 'k_rope': k_rope}

        # Decompress K/V from latent (training path / non-absorbed inference path)
        K = self.k_up(c_kv).view(B, -1, H, D)
        V = self.v_up(c_kv).view(B, -1, H, D)
        K_rope = k_rope.unsqueeze(2).expand(-1, -1, H, -1)  # broadcast across heads

        # Concatenate non-RoPE and RoPE channels for Q and K
        q_full = torch.cat([q, q_rope], dim=-1)                   # (B, T,    H, D + d_rope)
        k_full = torch.cat([K, K_rope], dim=-1)                   # (B, T_kv, H, D + d_rope)

        # Attention
        q_t = q_full.transpose(1, 2)                              # (B, H, T,    D + d_rope)
        k_t = k_full.transpose(1, 2)                              # (B, H, T_kv, D + d_rope)
        v_t = V.transpose(1, 2)                                   # (B, H, T_kv, D)
        scores = (q_t @ k_t.transpose(-2, -1)) / (q_full.size(-1) ** 0.5)
        if mask is not None:
            scores = scores + mask
        attn = F.softmax(scores, dim=-1)
        out = (attn @ v_t).transpose(1, 2).contiguous().view(B, T, H * D)
        return self.out_proj(out), new_cache


# ---- demo: cache size accounting ----
torch.manual_seed(0)
d_model, n_heads, d_latent, d_rope = 512, 8, 64, 16
mla = MultiHeadLatentAttention(d_model, n_heads, d_latent, d_rope)

x = torch.randn(2, 10, d_model)
y, cache = mla(x)
print(f"output shape         : {y.shape}")
print(f"cache c_kv shape     : {cache['c_kv'].shape}    (per-token: {d_latent} floats)")
print(f"cache k_rope shape   : {cache['k_rope'].shape}  (per-token: {d_rope} floats)")

standard_kv_per_token = 2 * d_model            # K + V at full dim
mla_per_token         = d_latent + d_rope      # latent + small RoPE channel
print(f"standard MHA cache   : {standard_kv_per_token} floats / token")
print(f"MLA cache            : {mla_per_token} floats / token")
print(f"compression ratio    : {standard_kv_per_token / mla_per_token:.1f}x")

# Decode step using the cache
new_token = torch.randn(2, 1, d_model)
y2, cache = mla(new_token, cache=cache)
print(f"after 1 decode step  : c_kv now {cache['c_kv'].shape}")

references.title: DeepSeek-V3 Technical Report — refined MLA + decoupled RoPE

sections.title: Inference Speedups: Speculative Decoding
sections.content:
Autoregressive decoding has a structural problem: even on a beefy GPU, generating one token at a time leaves most of the silicon idle. The bottleneck is *memory bandwidth*, not compute — every step you load all the weights to compute one token. Arithmetic intensity is terrible. **Speculative decoding** fixes this by amortizing weight loads across multiple tokens. **The idea (Leviathan et al., Chen et al., 2023).** Use a small, fast "draft" model to propose `N` tokens (typically `N=4–8`), then run the big "target" model **once** on all `N` proposed tokens *in parallel* (a single forward pass — same memory load, far higher arithmetic intensity). Compare the target's distributions against the draft's; accept the longest prefix where they agree (with a probabilistic acceptance rule that preserves the target's exact output distribution), and re-sample at the first disagreement. ```mermaid flowchart LR P["prompt"] --> D["Draft model
(small, fast)
proposes 4 tokens"] D --> Tgt["Target model
(big)
1 forward pass over
all 4 proposals + 1"] Tgt --> V{"verify token by
token; accept while
p_target / p_draft ≥ rand"} V -- "accept all 4" --> Win["4 tokens in 1 big-model step"] V -- "accept 2, reject 3rd" --> Mix["2 tokens + 1 resample"] Mix --> P Win --> P ``` **Why it preserves the distribution.** This is the elegant part. If the draft proposes token `t` with probability `q(t)` and the target would have given it `p(t)`, accept `t` with probability `min(1, p(t)/q(t))`. On rejection, sample from a corrected residual distribution `max(0, p − q)/sum`. The math (Leviathan §2) shows the resulting samples are exactly distributed as `p`. **Speculative decoding is not approximate** — it's a lossless speedup. **Expected speedup.** Typical 2–3× wall-clock for chat models, more for predictable text (code, structured output) where the draft agrees more often. Diminishing returns past `N=5–6`: longer drafts mean more wasted work on rejections. The draft model needs to be ~10–20× cheaper than the target to be worth it; common pairing is a 1B draft for a 70B target. **Variants.** - **Medusa** (Cai et al., 2024): instead of a separate draft model, attach `K` extra LM heads to the target model that each predict positions `t+1, t+2, …` directly. No two-model dance — but the heads need fine-tuning. Self-speculation. - **EAGLE** / **EAGLE-2**: decode at the feature level, not token level, with a tiny autoregressive head over hidden states. Higher acceptance rates than Medusa. - **Lookahead decoding**: no draft at all — uses Jacobi iteration to guess multiple positions in parallel. Smaller speedups but no extra training. - **Tree speculative decoding** (Sequoia, SpecInfer): the draft proposes a *tree* of continuations and the target verifies all of them in one forward pass, accepting whichever branch wins. Squeezes more out of each big-model call. Every production serving stack (vLLM, TGI, llama.cpp) supports speculative decoding as a flag. It's the single biggest "free" inference speedup of 2024–2025.

references.title: Fast Inference from Transformers via Speculative Decoding (Leviathan et al., 2023)
Accelerating Large Language Model Decoding with Speculative Sampling (Chen et al., 2023)
Medusa: Simple LLM Inference Acceleration with Multiple Decoding Heads
EAGLE-2: Faster Inference via Dynamic Draft Trees

sections.title: Serving Stacks in 2025
sections.content:
You've trained a model. How do you actually serve it fast? The 2025 ecosystem has consolidated around four serving stacks, each optimized for a different deployment shape. Knowing which to reach for is as important as knowing transformer internals.

**vLLM (CUDA / production GPU serving).** The dominant open-source GPU server. Two big innovations: **PagedAttention** treats the KV cache like virtual memory pages, allowing non-contiguous allocation and eliminating fragmentation (a problem that wasted ~60% of KV cache memory in earlier servers). **Continuous batching** dynamically merges new requests into the running batch every step, instead of waiting for the slowest request to finish. Result: 10–24× higher throughput than naive HF `generate()` at the same latency. Also ships speculative decoding, prefix caching, LoRA hot-swapping, and quantized inference (AWQ, GPTQ, FP8). **Pick this if:** you're deploying on NVIDIA GPUs and care about throughput.

**llama.cpp (CPU + GPU, GGUF format).** A C++ implementation focused on running quantized models everywhere — laptops, Raspberry Pis, phones. Custom **GGUF** weight format (Q4_K_M, Q5_K_M, Q8_0 quantizations) packs models into surprisingly small files: Llama-3-8B fits in ~5 GB at Q4_K_M with minimal quality loss. Backends: CPU (AVX-512), CUDA, Metal, ROCm, Vulkan. Lower throughput than vLLM but runs on a MacBook. **Pick this if:** you want local single-user inference with the broadest hardware coverage.

**MLX (Apple Silicon).** Apple's array framework, designed around unified memory: CPU and GPU share RAM, no host-device copies. The MLX-LM project ships optimized inference for Llama, Qwen, Mistral, DeepSeek, etc. on M-series Macs. Faster than llama.cpp on Apple hardware because it uses Metal directly with shape-specialized kernels, and the unified-memory model means quantization and weight-streaming tricks work naturally. **Pick this if:** you have Apple Silicon and want the best local inference speed.

**Text Generation Inference (TGI) / TensorRT-LLM (production stacks).** TGI is HuggingFace's production server (Rust + Python), used internally at HF for the Inference API; comparable feature set to vLLM with tighter HF integration. **TensorRT-LLM** is NVIDIA's officially supported stack — fastest on NVIDIA hardware (custom kernels, FP8 on Hopper, in-flight batching) but proprietary and harder to extend. **Pick TGI** for HF ecosystem fit; **TensorRT-LLM** for absolute peak performance on H100/H200/B200.

**Quick chooser:**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
  Q["What hardware?"] --> A{"Apple Silicon?"}
  A -- yes --> MLX["MLX-LM"]
  A -- no --> B{"Have NVIDIA GPU?"}
  B -- no --> LCPP["llama.cpp (CPU/Metal/ROCm/Vulkan)"]
  B -- yes --> C{"Single user or multi-tenant?"}
  C -- single --> LCPP2["llama.cpp (CUDA backend)"]
  C -- multi --> D{"HF ecosystem?"}
  D -- yes --> TGI["TGI"]
  D -- no --> E{"Need absolute peak?"}
  E -- yes --> TRT["TensorRT-LLM"]
  E -- no --> VLLM["vLLM"]
```

**Throughput vs latency.** Production deployments care about both. vLLM's continuous batching trades some single-request latency for big throughput wins; for chat UX (where time-to-first-token matters more than total throughput), tune the batch size and consider speculative decoding to cut TPOT (time per output token). On Apple Silicon, MLX is usually faster than llama.cpp for both metrics; on NVIDIA, vLLM beats TGI by ~10–20% in throughput, TensorRT-LLM beats both by another 20–40% but at the cost of build complexity.

references.title: vLLM: Easy, Fast, and Cheap LLM Serving with PagedAttention (Kwon et al., SOSP 2023)

sections.title: Measuring Model Quality: Eval Harnesses
sections.content:
Knowing a model exists is useless without knowing how it compares. The community has standardized on a small handful of benchmark suites and a single tool to run them all.

**The standard benchmarks.**
- **MMLU** — 57 multiple-choice subjects (high school to professional). The default "general knowledge" headline number. ~85% is human expert; current frontier models sit ~85–90%.
- **GSM8K** — grade-school math word problems. Tests multi-step reasoning. Frontier models ~95%+; chain-of-thought prompting helps a lot.
- **MATH** — competition-level math. Much harder than GSM8K. Frontier ~70–85%.
- **HumanEval / MBPP** — Python code generation. pass@1 is the headline. Frontier ~90%+.
- **BBH (Big-Bench Hard)** — 23 challenging reasoning tasks. Diverse, harder to game.
- **HellaSwag, ARC, WinoGrande** — common-sense reasoning, used heavily for smaller-model leaderboards.
- **HELM** (Stanford) — a meta-benchmark covering many tasks with standardized prompting and metrics. The most rigorous suite for academic claims.

**`lm-evaluation-harness` (EleutherAI).** The de facto standard tool for running these. Supports HF transformers, vLLM, OpenAI-compatible APIs, and many more backends. One command to evaluate any model on any subset of the above:

```bash
lm_eval --model hf --model_args pretrained=meta-llama/Llama-3.2-1B \
        --tasks mmlu,gsm8k,humaneval --device cuda:0 --batch_size 8
```

All the leaderboards (HF Open LLM Leaderboard, MTEB for embeddings, etc.) are basically `lm-eval` runs. If you ship a model, run it through `lm-eval` so your numbers are comparable.

**Contamination is a real problem.** Many web-scale pretraining corpora include the test sets verbatim. Models that look impressive on MMLU sometimes turn out to have memorized it. Mitigations: hold-out checks (test on private rephrasings), canary-string detection, and newer benchmarks designed to be hard to leak (**MMLU-Pro**, **GPQA**, **LiveBench** — refreshed monthly). When you read "model X gets 89% on MMLU," mentally subtract 2–5 points for likely contamination unless the team published a contamination audit.

**Beyond static benchmarks.** Chatbot Arena (LMSYS) does pairwise human-preference voting and is the most-cited "vibes" leaderboard. AlpacaEval, MT-Bench, and Arena-Hard use a stronger LLM as judge — cheaper than humans, decent correlation, but biased toward the judge's style. For agentic / tool-use evaluation: SWE-bench (real GitHub bug fixes), GAIA, WebArena.

If you only learn one tool from this section: it's `lm-eval`. If you only remember one rule: never trust a benchmark number that doesn't have a contamination audit.

references.title: MMLU: Measuring Massive Multitask Language Understanding
HELM: Holistic Evaluation of Language Models (Liang et al., 2022)
MMLU-Pro: A More Robust and Challenging Multi-Task Benchmark

sections.title: The Modern Training Pipeline (Course Wrap)
sections.content:
MoE, MLA, speculative decoding, vLLM, eval harnesses — none of them are useful in isolation. Here's how the pieces fit into the full 2025 model lifecycle, so you know where the techniques from this course land. ```mermaid flowchart LR C["Web-scale corpus
(trillions of tokens)"] --> PT["Pre-training
next-token prediction
(Modules 5-8 + this module)"] PT --> Base["Base model
(e.g. Llama-3 base)"] Base --> SFT["SFT
supervised fine-tuning
on (prompt, response) pairs"] SFT --> Inst["Instruct model"] Inst --> RM["Reward model
(human prefs) or DPO loss"] RM --> RL["RLHF / DPO / KTO
policy update"] Inst --> RL RL --> Final["Aligned model
(ChatGPT, Claude, Llama-Instruct)"] Final --> Eval["lm-eval, Arena, MT-Bench"] Final --> Serve["vLLM / llama.cpp / MLX
+ speculative decoding"] classDef stage fill:#1f6feb,stroke:#58a6ff,color:#fff class PT,SFT,RL stage ``` **Pre-training** is what this course mostly taught: the architecture (Modules 4–6), modern blocks like RMSNorm/SwiGLU/RoPE/GQA (Module 8), and now MoE + MLA for trillion-param scale. **SFT** trains on curated `(prompt, response)` pairs to make the base model follow instructions; chat templates (ChatML, Llama-3 format) define the structure. **RLHF** (or its simpler cousin **DPO**, **KTO**) fine-tunes on human preferences — usually pairs of "this response is better than that one." At **inference time**, the techniques from the second half of this module take over: serve via vLLM/llama.cpp/MLX, accelerate with speculative decoding, and measure quality with `lm-eval` plus Chatbot Arena. **What's *not* in this course but should be on your reading list:** tokenization (BPE/SentencePiece — step zero of every LLM), data pipelines (memmapped corpora, the nanoGPT pattern), distributed training (DDP, FSDP, expert parallelism for MoE), LoRA/QLoRA (the dominant 2025 fine-tuning recipe), `torch.profiler` for performance debugging, and the HuggingFace ecosystem (`transformers`, `peft`, `trl`, `datasets`, `accelerate`). These are practitioner gaps the architecture-focused curriculum doesn't fully cover — chase them next.

quiz.question: In a Mixture of Experts layer with 128 experts and top-2 routing, approximately what fraction of expert parameters are active per token?
quiz.explanation: Top-k routing activates exactly k experts out of N per token. Active / Total = k/N = 2/128 ≈ 1.5625%, which rounds to 1.6%. The other options correspond to k=1, a different N, or a misreading of "top-2" as a fraction.

quiz.question: Why is load balancing important in MoE, and what makes a *usable* (differentiable) load-balance loss?
options: Without balancing, the router collapses to a few favored experts; a usable loss multiplies a hard 'load' fraction by the soft 'importance' (mean router probs), giving the router gradients via importance.
Without balancing, GPU memory leaks; the loss is the variance of `router_probs > 0`, which is always > 0 after softmax.
Without balancing, gradients overflow; any positive scalar penalty works because the auxiliary loss is just a scaling constant.
Balancing prevents NaN values during training; counting `top_k_indices` directly via `torch.var` gives an exact differentiable signal.
quiz.explanation: If the router favors a few experts, the rest receive no gradients and become 'dead.' The Switch/GShard auxiliary loss multiplies `load[e]` (fraction of tokens routing to e — non-differentiable on its own) by `importance[e]` (mean router probability for e — soft, differentiable). The product gives the router a usable gradient via importance. Counting top-k indices directly is non-differentiable (option D) and `router_probs > 0` after softmax is always True so its variance is identically 0 (option B — this was a real bug in an earlier version of this module's code).

quiz.question: What is the *primary* advantage of Multi-head Latent Attention (MLA) over standard MHA, and what is the inference-time trick that makes it work?
options: It eliminates the need for positional encoding by learning relative positions in the latent.
It compresses K/V into a small shared latent that is cached, and at decode time the up-projection W_uk is absorbed into W_q so the latent is never decompressed — saving both memory and compute.
It replaces softmax with a linear approximation, reducing FLOPs without a memory benefit.
It shares Q across all heads, reducing query parameter count by a factor of n_heads.
quiz.explanation: MLA caches a compressed latent c_kv (e.g., 512 dims) instead of full K and V (e.g., 4096 dims each), giving ~16-32× cache compression. Critically, the matrix-absorption trick at inference (W_q · W_uk^T precomputed offline) means the latent is never expanded — attention scores are computed directly between the query and the compressed latent, saving compute as well as memory. RoPE is handled separately via a small decoupled-RoPE channel. The other options describe unrelated techniques.

quiz.question: Speculative decoding speeds up inference. Which statement about it is correct?
options: It's an approximate technique: the output distribution is close to but not equal to greedy decoding from the target model.
It runs the target model on N draft-proposed tokens in parallel, accepts a prefix using a probabilistic rule, and provably samples from the *exact* target distribution.
It only helps when the draft and target model use the same architecture and weights.
Its speedup grows monotonically with N (longer drafts always help).
quiz.explanation: Speculative decoding (Leviathan, Chen et al. 2023) is *lossless*: the rejection-sampling rule (accept with probability min(1, p/q), resample from max(0, p-q) on rejection) produces samples exactly distributed according to the target. The win is that one big-model forward pass over N tokens has the same memory-bandwidth cost as a single decode step but produces multiple tokens worth of verification. Speedup is typically 2-3× and saturates around N=5-6 because rejection wastes proposed tokens; the draft can have any architecture as long as it's small/fast enough.

quiz.question: You're deploying a Llama-3 8B model for multi-tenant chat on an NVIDIA A100. Which serving stack should you reach for first, and why?
options: llama.cpp, because GGUF Q4_K_M quantization fits on smaller GPUs.
vLLM, because PagedAttention eliminates KV-cache fragmentation and continuous batching merges new requests every step — yielding 10-24× higher throughput than naive HF generate().
MLX-LM, because it has the lowest latency on unified-memory systems.
Pure HuggingFace `transformers.generate()`, because it's the most ergonomic and the throughput difference is negligible.
quiz.explanation: vLLM is the dominant open-source GPU server in 2025. PagedAttention treats KV cache like virtual memory pages (no fragmentation), and continuous batching merges new requests dynamically instead of waiting for the slowest request — typical 10-24× throughput improvement for multi-tenant workloads. MLX-LM is for Apple Silicon, llama.cpp targets the broadest hardware (best for laptops/edge), and naive `transformers.generate()` leaves most of the GPU idle. TensorRT-LLM may beat vLLM by another 20-40% but at the cost of build complexity.

exercise.title: Build a Simple MoE Layer with a *Working* Load-Balance Loss
exercise.description: Implement a basic MoE layer with top-k routing and — critically — a differentiable load-balance auxiliary loss that actually trains the router. Create 4-8 experts, route tokens using softmax + top-k, and verify the load-balance loss is non-zero and produces non-zero gradients on the router weights. Then test that under a synthetic 'preferred-expert' input distribution, the loss penalizes the imbalance.
exercise.starterCode:
import torch
import torch.nn as nn

class Expert(nn.Module):

        pass

    def forward(self, x):
        # TODO: Forward pass through expert
        pass

class SimpleMoE(nn.Module):
    """Simple Mixture of Experts with top-k routing."""

        super().__init__()
        # TODO: Initialize experts, router, and parameters
        self.num_experts = num_experts
        self.top_k = top_k
        self.aux_weight = aux_weight
        self.aux_loss = torch.tensor(0.0)

    def forward(self, x):
        """
        Args:
            x: (batch, seq_len, d_model)

        Returns:
            output: (batch, seq_len, d_model)
        """

        # 1. Flatten to (T, d_model) and compute router probs
        # 2. Take top_k indices and renormalize their probs
        # 3. Dispatch tokens through each expert and combine
        # 4. Compute the *differentiable* load-balance loss:
        #       importance[e] = router_probs.mean(dim=0)         # soft
        #       load[e]       = one_hot(top_k_idx).sum(1).mean(0) # hard
        #       aux = top_k * num_experts * (load * importance).sum()
        # 5. Return output reshaped back to (batch, seq_len, d_model)
        pass

if __name__ == '__main__':
    d_model, num_experts, d_ff, top_k = 256, 4, 512, 2
    moe = SimpleMoE(d_model, num_experts, d_ff, top_k)
    x = torch.randn(4, 8, d_model)

    output = moe(x)
    print(f"Output shape: {output.shape}")
    print(f"Aux loss: {moe.aux_loss.item():.6f}")

exercise.solution:
import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import torch.nn.functional as F

class Expert(nn.Module):
    """Single expert FFN."""
    def __init__(self, d_model, d_ff):
        super().__init__()
        self.w1 = nn.Linear(d_model, d_ff)
        self.w2 = nn.Linear(d_ff, d_model)

    def forward(self, x):
        return self.w2(F.gelu(self.w1(x)))

class SimpleMoE(nn.Module):
    """Simple Mixture of Experts with top-k routing and a differentiable
    Switch/GShard-style load-balance auxiliary loss."""
    def __init__(self, d_model, num_experts, d_ff, top_k, aux_weight=0.01):
        super().__init__()
        self.num_experts = num_experts
        self.top_k = top_k
        self.aux_weight = aux_weight

        self.experts = nn.ModuleList([Expert(d_model, d_ff) for _ in range(num_experts)])
        self.router = nn.Linear(d_model, num_experts, bias=False)
        self.aux_loss = torch.tensor(0.0)

    def forward(self, x):
        B, T, D = x.shape
        x_flat = x.view(-1, D)                              # (T_total, D)

        # 1-2. Router and top-k
        router_probs = F.softmax(self.router(x_flat), dim=-1)     # (T, N)
        top_k_probs, top_k_idx = torch.topk(router_probs, self.top_k, dim=-1)
        top_k_probs = top_k_probs / top_k_probs.sum(dim=-1, keepdim=True)

        # 3. Dispatch and combine
        out = torch.zeros_like(x_flat)
        for k in range(self.top_k):
            for e in range(self.num_experts):
                mask = (top_k_idx[:, k] == e)
                if mask.any():
                    out[mask] += top_k_probs[mask, k:k+1] * self.experts[e](x_flat[mask])

        # 4. Differentiable load-balance loss
        importance = router_probs.mean(dim=0)                       # soft, has grad
        topk_one_hot = F.one_hot(top_k_idx, num_classes=self.num_experts).sum(dim=1)
        load = topk_one_hot.float().mean(dim=0)                     # hard, no grad through it
        aux = self.top_k * self.num_experts * (load * importance).sum()
        self.aux_loss = self.aux_weight * aux

        return out.view(B, T, D)

if __name__ == '__main__':
    torch.manual_seed(0)
    d_model, num_experts, d_ff, top_k = 256, 4, 512, 2
    moe = SimpleMoE(d_model, num_experts, d_ff, top_k)
    x = torch.randn(4, 8, d_model)

    output = moe(x)
    assert output.shape == x.shape
    print(f"Output shape: {output.shape}")
    print(f"Aux loss: {moe.aux_loss.item():.6f}")

    # Check that gradients flow into router weights via the aux loss alone
    moe.zero_grad()
    moe.aux_loss.backward(retain_graph=True)
    grad_norm = moe.router.weight.grad.abs().sum().item()
    print(f"Router gradient L1 from aux loss: {grad_norm:.6f}")
    assert grad_norm > 0, "Aux loss must produce non-zero router gradients!"

    # Param accounting
    expert_params = sum(p.numel() for p in moe.experts.parameters())
    router_params = sum(p.numel() for p in moe.router.parameters())
    print(f"Total expert params: {expert_params/1e6:.2f}M")
    print(f"Active per token   : {(top_k/num_experts):.1%}")

    # Imbalance test: under uniform routing the floor is top_k/N = 0.5; collapse spikes it.
    # Force-collapse the router by setting most logits very negative for all but one expert.
    with torch.no_grad():
        moe.router.weight.zero_()
        moe.router.weight[0] += 10.0   # bias toward expert 0 strongly
    moe.zero_grad()
    _ = moe(x)
    print(f"Aux loss under collapse: {moe.aux_loss.item():.6f}  (should be > uniform)")

    print("\u2713 MoE test passed!")
